answers.text: (1) ping 8.8.8.8 — sends ICMP echo requests and reports round-trip time and packet loss confirming basic IP reachability; (2) traceroute 8.8.8.8 (Linux/Mac) or tracert 8.8.8.8 (Windows) — sends packets with incrementing TTL values revealing each hop and its round-trip time, identifying where latency increases or where packets stop returning (indicating the drop point); (3) netstat -an or ss -tlnp (Linux) — shows all network connections and listening ports on the local system identifying which services are accepting connections on which ports; (4) nslookup google.com or dig google.com — queries DNS and returns the resolved IP address(es) for the hostname
(1) nslookup 8.8.8.8, (2) netstat, (3) dig, (4) ping google.com — these are the standard four commands for these tasks
(1) arp, (2) ipconfig, (3) ping, (4) traceroute — these four commands serve the described functions in the same order
(1) netstat, (2) nslookup, (3) traceroute, (4) ping — each command serves one specific function only and cannot be used for any other purpose

explanations.a: Ping for reachability, traceroute for path analysis and drop identification, netstat for local port visibility, and nslookup/dig for DNS resolution are the four correct command-to-function mappings. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: The assignments are incorrect; ping tests connectivity not nslookup; netstat shows ports not path information; dig resolves DNS not ping.
explanations.c: The assignments are incorrect; arp shows ARP table not test connectivity; ipconfig shows local IP configuration not path information.
explanations.d: The assignments are incorrect; netstat shows connections not test connectivity; nslookup resolves DNS not trace paths.

questions.text: A security engineer captures traffic on a Linux server using tcpdump and also wants to query DNS directly to test resolution. They use 'tcpdump -i eth0 port 53' and 'dig @8.8.8.8 example.com'. What does each command do specifically and what information does each provide that the other cannot?

answers.text: tcpdump and dig perform identical functions; both capture DNS traffic; dig is a graphical version of tcpdump designed for DNS specifically; use either tool interchangeably for DNS investigation

tcpdump -i eth0 port 53 only captures traffic on port 53 UDP; DNS over HTTPS (DoH) on port 443 and DNS over TLS (DoT) on port 853 are invisible to this command; dig @8.8.8.8 only works when UDP port 53 is not blocked; both tools fail when modern encrypted DNS is deployed

tcpdump -i eth0 port 53 captures all DNS packets (queries and responses) on interface eth0 in real time showing the raw network traffic including source IP, destination IP, DNS query type, queried name, and response contents for ALL DNS activity on the system from all processes simultaneously; it reveals what the system is actually querying versus what applications believe they are querying and captures the complete packet including any anomalies; dig @8.8.8.8 example.com directly queries the DNS server at 8.8.8.8 (Google Public DNS) for example.com and displays the structured response including the answer section (resolved IPs), authority section (nameservers), additional section (glue records), query time, and which server answered; dig tests a specific resolver directly without going through system resolver settings; tcpdump cannot initiate DNS queries (it only captures) and dig cannot capture all system DNS traffic (it only makes a specific query); together they provide both visibility into system-wide DNS behavior and targeted resolver testing

The @8.8.8.8 parameter in dig specifies the source IP address of the query not the destination resolver; dig queries the system default resolver and tags packets with source IP 8.8.8.8; this is used to test firewall rules that filter by source IP

explanations.a: tcpdump and dig have fundamentally different functions; tcpdump is a passive packet capture tool while dig is an active DNS query tool; they are complementary not interchangeable.
explanations.b: While the observation about DoH/DoT is technically correct it does not describe the primary functions of either tool accurately; the main distinction is passive capture vs. active query not protocol encryption.
explanations.c: The passive capture (tcpdump seeing all system DNS traffic) vs. active query (dig testing a specific resolver) distinction with their unique information contributions is the complete answer; together they provide comprehensive DNS troubleshooting capability. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: The @8.8.8.8 parameter in dig specifies the resolver to query (the destination DNS server) not the source IP; it overrides the system default resolver for that specific query.

questions.text: A network engineer runs 'nmap -sV -p 22,80,443,3389 192.168.1.0/24' on an internal subnet. What does each flag do, what information does the scan provide, and what security policy considerations apply to running Nmap on a production network?

answers.text: Nmap is only legal to run against external internet IP addresses; scanning internal networks is illegal regardless of whether the engineer owns the network; the engineer should use ping sweep instead of Nmap on internal subnets

Nmap scans require root or administrator privileges on the scanning machine but produce no traffic on the network; the scan works by querying the switch MAC address table locally rather than sending packets to target hosts; no authorization is needed because the scan generates no network traffic

The -sV flag instructs Nmap to only scan virtual machines and skip physical servers; the -p flag specifies the scanning priority level (higher numbers scan first); the scan creates a complete backup of all 254 hosts on the subnet for disaster recovery purposes

Command breakdown: -sV performs service version detection — after finding open ports Nmap probes them to identify the specific application and version (e.g., OpenSSH 8.9 on port 22, Apache 2.4.54 on port 80); -p 22,80,443,3389 restricts the scan to those four specific ports (SSH, HTTP, HTTPS, RDP) rather than scanning all 65,535 ports; 192.168.1.0/24 targets all 254 hosts on the subnet; the scan provides: which hosts are up and responding, which of the four ports are open on each host, and what service and version is running on each open port; this is valuable for security auditing (finding unexpected open ports or unpatched service versions), asset inventory, and vulnerability assessment; security policy considerations: Nmap service version scanning generates network traffic that IDS/SIEM systems will detect and alert on; scanning should be authorized in the change management system and scheduled during low-impact windows; scanning may trigger false-positive security alerts requiring SOC notification in advance; some IPS configurations block aggressive scanning causing the scan itself to be disrupted; written authorization from the system owner is required before scanning production systems even on internal networks; document the scan results in the asset inventory

explanations.a: Network engineers routinely scan internal networks they own and manage; Nmap is a standard network management tool; the legal and authorization question relates to authorization not internal vs. external distinction.
explanations.b: Nmap absolutely sends packets to target hosts; the SYN scan (-sS) sends TCP SYN packets; the service version detection (-sV) sends application-layer probes; Nmap generates network traffic that is visible to IDS and switch logs.
explanations.c: The flag descriptions are completely incorrect; -sV means service version detection and -p means port specification; Nmap does not create backups.
explanations.d: Accurate flag description (-sV for version detection, -p for port specification), information produced (host status, open ports, service versions), and security considerations (IDS detection, authorization requirement, SOC notification) are all correct. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: A network technician on a Linux workstation needs to: find the workstation's current IP, gateway, and MAC address; view the ARP cache; and check if port 443 is listening locally. What specific commands accomplish each task?

answers.text: Use ping for IP address, traceroute for gateway, nmap for MAC address, netstat for ARP cache, and dig for listening ports — these five commands cover all five needs

ip addr show (or ifconfig on older systems) — displays all interfaces with their IP addresses, MAC addresses (listed as ether), and network masks; ip route show (or route -n) — displays the routing table including the default route which identifies the default gateway (line beginning with 0.0.0.0 or default); arp -n or ip neighbor show — displays the ARP cache showing IP-to-MAC mappings that the workstation has recently learned; ss -tlnp or netstat -tlnp — shows TCP listening ports (-t for TCP, -l for listening, -n for numeric, -p for process name); filtering for port 443 with 'ss -tlnp | grep 443' or 'netstat -tlnp | grep 443' shows whether a process is listening on that port; on Windows the equivalents are ipconfig /all (IP+MAC), route print (gateway), arp -a (ARP cache), and netstat -an | findstr :443 (port check)

Use a single command 'nmap localhost' which displays all IP addresses, MAC addresses, gateway information, ARP cache entries, and listening ports in one output; Nmap replaces all other diagnostic commands for local system information
Use the dig command for all five needs; dig supports special queries that return IP address, gateway, MAC address, ARP cache, and port status when combined with the appropriate record type options

explanations.a: The command assignments are incorrect; ping does not show IP configuration; traceroute does not show gateway (beyond finding the first hop); nmap does not directly show MAC addresses through local configuration.
explanations.b: ip addr, ip route, arp/ip neighbor, and ss/netstat are the correct commands for local IP configuration, gateway, ARP cache, and listening port inspection respectively; the Windows equivalents are also correctly provided. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: Nmap targets network hosts and does not replace ip addr, ip route, arp, and netstat for local system information; Nmap requires root for raw socket access and is a network scanner not a local configuration tool.
explanations.d: Dig is a DNS query tool that resolves hostnames to IPs; it cannot display local IP configuration, ARP cache, or listening ports; it serves only DNS query functions.

questions.text: A network technician needs to trace an unlabeled cable through a wall and also check if a fiber cable is broken. Which tools are needed for each task?

answers.text: A toner (tone generator and inductive probe or amplifier probe) traces the unlabeled copper cable: the tone generator connects to one end of the cable and transmits an audio tone; the probe held near cables detects the tone through the cable jacket and insulation allowing the technician to follow the cable through walls and to the correct patch panel port without needing physical access to both ends simultaneously; a visual fault locator (VFL) — a device that injects a visible red laser light (635-650nm) into a fiber optic cable — is used to check for breaks: the red light is visible at any break point, crack, or damaged connector in the fiber where light leaks out; an intact fiber carries the light to the far end without visible leakage; both tools are handheld, inexpensive, and do not require powered network infrastructure to operate
A toner (tone generator and probe) for both tasks; toners work on all cable types including fiber optic and are the universal cable tracing and testing tool for all network media
A Wi-Fi analyzer for both tasks; Wi-Fi analyzers can detect all cable types using RF signal analysis and also test fiber continuity by measuring 2.4 GHz signal attenuation through the cable

A cable tester for both tasks; cable testers simultaneously test all cable types including fiber and copper and can trace cables through walls using the built-in tone generation feature that works on fiber optic cables

explanations.a: Toner/probe for copper cable tracing and visual fault locator for fiber break detection are the correct tool-to-task mappings; each tool is designed for its specific media type and purpose. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: Toners work through electromagnetic induction and require copper conductors; they cannot generate a traceable tone in fiber optic cables which carry light not electrical signals.
explanations.c: Wi-Fi analyzers measure radio frequency signals in the air; they cannot trace cables or test fiber continuity.
explanations.d: Standard cable testers verify wire map and continuity but do not function as toners for in-wall tracing; cable testers require access to both ends simultaneously; they cannot trace fiber breaks through visual inspection.

questions.text: A SOC analyst needs to capture all packets on a 10 Gbps link between a web server and the firewall for security analysis without impacting traffic flow. A network TAP is deployed. How does a TAP differ from a SPAN port and why is it preferred for security monitoring?

answers.text: A TAP and SPAN port are identical technologies with different names; choose whichever is available on the network equipment as they provide exactly the same packet capture quality

A TAP is used only for fiber optic links while SPAN ports work only with copper cables; the media type determines which technology is appropriate; the SOC should use a TAP only if the web server connection uses fiber

A network TAP (Test Access Point) is a passive hardware device inserted inline on the physical cable between the web server and firewall; it creates a physical copy of all electrical or optical signals on the link and sends them to the monitoring port; TAPs are preferred over SPAN ports for security monitoring because: TAPs capture 100% of traffic with zero packet loss even when the link is at 100% utilization (SPAN ports drop packets when the monitor port cannot keep up with aggregate source traffic); TAPs are fully passive and transparent — they have no IP address, cannot be detected, targeted, or compromised by attackers on the monitored network, and continue functioning if the network switch fails or reboots; TAPs capture both directions of traffic as separate streams preserving timing relationships (some SPAN implementations merge both directions onto one output introducing timing artifacts); TAPs do not consume switch ASIC resources (SPAN processing impacts switch performance at high traffic volumes); the trade-off is that TAPs require physical inline installation (cutting the cable to insert the TAP) which requires a maintenance window; SPAN is configured in software on existing switches without physical changes

TAPs actively filter traffic before sending it to the monitoring port removing duplicate packets and error frames; SPAN ports pass raw unfiltered traffic; TAPs provide cleaner captures while SPAN provides more complete captures; for security analysis the filtered TAP output is preferred

explanations.a: TAPs and SPAN ports have meaningful technical differences including packet loss behavior, switch resource consumption, detectability, and full-duplex stream handling; they are not interchangeable.
explanations.b: TAPs are available for both fiber optic (optical TAPs) and copper (electrical TAPs) links; SPAN ports work on both media types as well; the choice is not determined by cable type.
explanations.c: Zero packet loss, passive transparency, switch independence, and full-duplex stream preservation are the four key advantages of TAPs over SPAN for security monitoring; the physical installation trade-off is accurately described. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: TAPs are passive and do not filter traffic; they provide exact copies of everything on the link including all errors and malformed frames; SPAN ports may drop packets under high load but do not filter traffic deliberately.

questions.text: A network engineer deploys a Wi-Fi analyzer in a healthcare facility and discovers 15 overlapping SSIDs on channels 1, 6, and 11 with signal strengths between -45 dBm and -75 dBm. The 2.4 GHz spectrum shows 80% channel utilization on channel 6. What does this data indicate and what remediation does the analyzer inform?

answers.text: 15 SSIDs on three channels is optimal wireless design; all 2.4 GHz channels should be occupied to ensure maximum coverage throughout the facility; 80% channel utilization confirms all three channels are being used efficiently

The analyzer data is inconclusive; Wi-Fi analyzers cannot reliably measure channel utilization in healthcare facilities because medical equipment generates electromagnetic interference that corrupts the measurement; repeat the analysis in a non-medical environment to get accurate readings

The 80% channel 6 utilization is caused by a rogue access point broadcasting on channel 6; disable all other APs and identify the rogue AP using its BSSID in the analyzer output; removing the rogue AP will restore channel utilization to 0%

The Wi-Fi analyzer data reveals significant wireless problems: 80% channel utilization on channel 6 means 80% of available airtime is consumed — with multiple SSIDs competing for the same channel, clients experience significant retry rates, reduced throughput, and latency; even with strong signal (-45 dBm) devices on channel 6 perform poorly because they must wait for the channel to be available before transmitting (CSMA/CA contention); the -75 dBm signals are near the -80 dBm noise floor meaning those APs barely cover their areas; in a healthcare facility with medical devices on 2.4 GHz (some wireless medical equipment) the high contention directly impacts reliability of patient monitoring systems; remediation informed by the analyzer: migrate capacity to 5 GHz (which has 25 non-overlapping channels at 20 MHz and is far less congested in healthcare facilities) for all non-legacy devices; audit which systems require 2.4 GHz and minimize 2.4 GHz AP transmit power to reduce coverage overlap and co-channel contention; verify that channels 1, 6, and 11 are properly assigned with no overlap; evaluate whether some APs should be disabled or relocated based on the signal strength map; implement 802.11r/k/v for proper roaming as the density increases

explanations.a: 15 SSIDs on three channels means many APs are co-channel competing on each channel; this is poor wireless design causing contention; 80% channel utilization is too high for reliable performance.
explanations.b: Wi-Fi analyzers do measure channel utilization accurately in healthcare settings; medical equipment may appear as interference sources in the spectrum analysis which is actually useful data; the analyzer works correctly in these environments.
explanations.c: Multiple SSIDs on the same channel are expected in dense deployments; the high utilization is caused by the aggregate of all these APs competing not a single rogue; a rogue AP is one possible contributor but the wholesale analysis shows a systemic density problem.
explanations.d: Co-channel interference analysis, the impact on healthcare reliability, and the 5 GHz migration remediation are all correct; Wi-Fi analyzers specifically provide the channel utilization and signal data needed to diagnose these exact conditions. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: A network engineer troubleshooting a connectivity issue needs to: see the MAC address table on a switch, check which VLANs are configured, and view the ARP cache on a router. What show commands provide this information?

answers.text: show mac address-table — displays the switch's CAM table mapping MAC addresses to switch ports and VLANs; this helps identify which physical port a specific device is connected to and is used to trace traffic paths through the switch fabric; show vlan — displays all configured VLANs with their ID, name, status (active/suspended), and which ports are assigned to each VLAN; this verifies correct VLAN configuration and port assignments; show arp — displays the router's ARP cache mapping IP addresses to MAC addresses for directly connected devices; this helps verify that the router has correctly learned the MAC address of devices on its connected subnets and is essential for diagnosing ARP-related connectivity issues
show ip interface for MAC table, show running-config for VLANs, show ip route for ARP cache — these three commands provide all the requested information
show interface for MAC table, show cdp neighbors for VLANs, show ip bgp for ARP cache — these commands provide the requested information on Cisco IOS devices
show power inline for MAC table, show spanning-tree for VLANs, show processes for ARP cache — these commands are the correct Cisco IOS syntax for the three requested information types

explanations.a: Show mac address-table for CAM table, show vlan for VLAN configuration, and show arp for the ARP cache are the three correct command mappings with accurate descriptions of what each provides. This is the correct answer.
explanations.b: The command assignments are incorrect; show ip interface shows IP configuration not MAC address tables; show running-config contains VLAN configuration but show vlan is the specific command; show ip route shows the routing table not ARP cache.
explanations.c: The assignments are incorrect; show interface shows interface statistics not MAC address tables; show cdp neighbors shows directly connected Cisco devices not VLAN configuration; show ip bgp shows BGP routes not ARP cache.
explanations.d: All three command assignments are incorrect; show power inline shows PoE power status not MAC table; show spanning-tree shows STP topology not VLAN configuration; show processes shows CPU processes not ARP cache.

questions.text: A network engineer investigates why a switch port is not forwarding traffic. They run show interface GigabitEthernet0/1 and see: 'GigabitEthernet0/1 is up, line protocol is down.' They also see 'output drops: 4521' on GigabitEthernet0/2. What does each output indicate and what is investigated next?

answers.text: GigabitEthernet0/1 up/line protocol down is normal during system startup; the port becomes fully operational after a 60-second initialization period; output drops on 0/2 indicate the interface is functioning correctly at maximum capacity

GigabitEthernet0/1 up/line protocol down means the port has been administratively disabled using the shutdown command; use no shutdown to re-enable it; the output drops on 0/2 indicate the interface has experienced 4521 physical cable disconnections

GigabitEthernet0/1 is up but line protocol is down indicates a Layer 1/Layer 2 mismatch: the physical signal is present (carrier detect on the cable — up) but the Layer 2 protocol (the data-link layer handshake confirming frame synchronization) has not established; common causes include: speed or duplex mismatch between the switch port and the connected device; the remote device is not sending keepalives or valid Layer 2 frames; a Layer 2 encapsulation mismatch (rare on Ethernet but occurs on WAN interfaces); or the remote device has a physical layer issue with its NIC generating signal but not valid frames; investigate by checking the connected device NIC status, verifying speed/duplex configuration on both sides (show interface shows configured speed and duplex), and testing with a known-good cable and device; the 4521 output drops on GigabitEthernet0/2 indicate that the interface's output queue is discarding packets because traffic is arriving faster than it can be transmitted — the egress interface is congested; investigate by checking interface utilization (show interface shows output rate) and determining if QoS is configured to manage the congestion

Both status messages are errors in the SNMP MIB display; the actual interface status can only be determined by physically inspecting the port LED; show interface output is unreliable for diagnosing real interface issues

explanations.a: Up/line protocol down is not a startup state; it persists until the Layer 2 issue is resolved; output drops indicate congestion not normal maximum operation.
explanations.b: Administratively disabled shows as administratively down not up/line protocol down; the two states have different show interface output; output drops count packet discards due to queue congestion not physical disconnections.
explanations.c: The up/line protocol down distinction (Layer 1 physical signal vs. Layer 2 protocol establishment) and output drops as egress congestion indicators are both correct interpretations; these are standard show interface diagnostic readings that network engineers must interpret correctly. This is the correct answer.
explanations.d: Show interface is the primary Cisco IOS tool for interface diagnostics; it provides accurate real-time status information; it is the standard and reliable method for diagnosing interface issues.

questions.text: A network engineer is troubleshooting a routing issue. They run show route on a router and see two routes to 10.50.0.0/16: one via OSPF with metric 110 and one via EIGRP with metric 90. They also run show config and find a static route to 10.50.0.0/16. What does route selection do with these three competing routes and which is installed in the forwarding table?

answers.text: The static route is always used when multiple routes exist to the same destination; static routes override all dynamic routing protocols regardless of metric or administrative distance

The EIGRP route with metric 90 is selected because 90 is lower than 110 (OSPF metric); the static route has no metric so it cannot be compared and is ignored; the static route is only used if EIGRP and OSPF routes are removed from the routing table

All three routes are simultaneously active and the router load-balances traffic equally across all three paths; this is called multi-protocol load balancing and is the default behavior when multiple routing sources have routes to the same destination

When multiple routes to the same destination exist from different sources the router uses Administrative Distance (AD) to select which route to install in the FIB (Forwarding Information Base) for actual packet forwarding; AD is a measure of the trustworthiness of the routing source with lower being more trusted; Cisco default ADs: static route = 1, EIGRP = 90, OSPF = 110; since static route has AD 1 (lower than both EIGRP 90 and OSPF 110) the static route is installed in the forwarding table and used for packet forwarding; EIGRP and OSPF routes are in the routing table but not in the active forwarding path; the metric values listed (110 for OSPF, 90 for EIGRP) are the route metrics within each protocol not the AD — OSPF metric of 110 and EIGRP metric of 90 would only be compared if both routes came from the same protocol (ECMP within one protocol); metrics are only compared between routes of the same AD; to verify which route is active use show ip route 10.50.0.0 which shows the installed route with an S (static), D (EIGRP), or O (OSPF) prefix indicating the source

explanations.a: Static routes with default AD of 1 do take precedence over OSPF (110) and EIGRP (90) but the statement that static routes always override is an oversimplification; a static route configured with a higher AD (called a floating static route) would not override dynamic routes; the specific AD values determine selection.
explanations.b: Metrics are only compared between routes from the same protocol; OSPF metric and EIGRP metric are not comparable (they use different calculation methods and scales); administrative distance is the selection criterion between protocols.
explanations.c: Multi-protocol load balancing across routes from different sources (static, EIGRP, OSPF) is not the default behavior; the route with lowest AD is selected as the single active route; equal-cost load balancing occurs only within one protocol when multiple equal-metric routes from the same source exist.
explanations.d: Administrative distance as the inter-protocol route selection criterion with the specific default AD values (static=1, EIGRP=90, OSPF=110) and the distinction between AD and metric (intra-protocol) is the complete and accurate answer. This is the correct answer.

questions.text: A network engineer runs show power inline on a Cisco PoE switch and sees: 'Available: 370.0(w) Used: 352.3(w) Remaining: 17.7(w).' An IP phone is not powering up on port Gi0/24. Show power inline Gi0/24 shows 'off.' What does this output indicate and what should be done?

answers.text: The power inline output is a display bug; there is always sufficient PoE power available regardless of what show power inline reports; reset the switch to refresh the power accounting and the phone will power up

The switch has only 17.7W of remaining PoE budget but standard IP phones (802.3af Class 2) require 6.49W and PoE+ phones (802.3at Class 4) require up to 30W; if the phone is PoE+ class the switch cannot allocate the required 30W from the remaining 17.7W and denies power to port Gi0/24 (shown as off); if the phone is standard 802.3af the 17.7W should be sufficient — in this case the off state may indicate: the phone MAC is not being detected (bad cable), PoE is administratively disabled on the port (power inline never on Gi0/24), or the phone is not drawing power correctly; solutions: verify the phone PoE class requirement and compare to remaining budget; if budget is insufficient remove or replace a high-wattage device elsewhere on the switch or add a PoE injector for the phone; if budget is sufficient check the port configuration (show run interface Gi0/24) for any PoE restriction and check the cable; power inline Gi0/24 auto can re-enable auto-negotiated PoE on the port

The remaining 17.7W indicates the phone is receiving 17.7W of power which is sufficient for all IP phone models; the off status in show power inline indicates the phone is in power-saving standby mode not that it is unpowered; wait 5 minutes for the phone to wake from standby
IP phones never use show power inline; show power inline only displays information for security cameras and wireless APs; use show cdp neighbors to verify IP phone PoE status

explanations.a: Show power inline is an accurate real-time display of PoE budget allocation; it is not a bug; resetting the switch would potentially drop all powered devices and not add power budget.
explanations.b: Comparing remaining budget to phone class requirement, identifying possible causes for off state when budget appears sufficient, and providing specific remediation steps are all correct diagnostic thinking. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: The remaining 17.7W is what is left in the switch budget not what the phone receives; off in power inline means the port is not currently supplying power; standby mode in an IP phone would still show active power consumption.
explanations.d: Show power inline is the correct command for PoE port status on all PoE-powered devices including IP phones; CDP neighbors shows device connectivity not PoE status.

questions.text: A network engineer uses show route on a router and finds no route to the destination 192.168.50.0/24 despite the remote site advertising it via OSPF. Show ip ospf neighbor shows the OSPF adjacency is full. What additional show commands help diagnose why the OSPF route is not appearing in the routing table?

answers.text: The route is missing because OSPF is not running; show route confirming no route means the OSPF process has failed; restart the OSPF process with no router ospf 1 followed by router ospf 1 and the route will appear

When OSPF adjacency is full but a specific route is missing from the routing table despite the remote site advertising it, several show commands help isolate the cause: show ip ospf database — displays the OSPF link-state database; if the Type 3 LSA (inter-area summary) or Type 1/2 LSA for 192.168.50.0/24 is absent from the database the route is not being advertised into this area despite adjacency being up (possible area type mismatch, area authentication failure, or summarization suppressing the route); show ip ospf database network — specifically shows network LSAs for multi-access networks; show ip route ospf — lists only OSPF-learned routes and confirms if the route is in the table under any variant; show run | section router ospf — shows the OSPF configuration including network statements (verify 192.168.50.x is included in a network statement on the advertising router), area configurations, and any distribute-list or route filtering that may be suppressing the route; common causes: a distribute-list or prefix-list filtering 192.168.50.0/24 from being installed; the route is in a stub area and the ABR is summarizing it away; the advertising router does not have the network statement covering 192.168.50.x; an OSPF area mismatch between the two routers

The route is missing because 192.168.50.0/24 is a private address range blocked by OSPF; OSPF cannot advertise RFC1918 private addresses between areas; use BGP instead of OSPF for RFC1918 route advertisement between sites

Show ip ospf neighbor showing full adjacency guarantees that all routes from that neighbor are in the local routing table; if a route is missing with full adjacency the route does not exist at the remote site; verify the remote site has a connected interface in 192.168.50.0/24

explanations.a: Full OSPF adjacency means the process is running; the issue is specific route advertisement or filtering not a process failure; restarting OSPF would drop all adjacencies causing a broader outage.
explanations.b: Show ip ospf database for LSA verification, show run for configuration audit including network statements and route filters, and the list of common causes (distribute-list, stub area, network statement, area mismatch) represent comprehensive OSPF troubleshooting methodology. This is the correct answer.
explanations.c: OSPF has no restriction on advertising RFC1918 private addresses; OSPF operates purely based on network statements and area configuration; address range type does not determine OSPF advertisement eligibility.
explanations.d: Full adjacency means the database exchange completed but does not guarantee all routes from that site appear in the local table; route filtering (distribute-lists, prefix-lists, area configurations) can suppress specific routes from being installed even with full adjacency.
